title: RA.986 Suze Ijó
tracklist:
Key Tronics Ensemble - Calypso of House (Paradise Mix)
Chaos In The CBD - Marlboro Sounds
Anthony Nicholson - Tribes Of Chant (World Dance) (Original Mix)
Lonesome Echo Production - Sweet Dream (Shrine Sweet Mix)
Vick Lavender  - 7 Rings of Saturn (Vick's Time Traveler Extended Mix)	
Doug Gomez ft. Ambrosia - HAPPY (Doug Gomez Vocal Rmx)	
Incognito - On The Road (Danny Krivit Re-Edit)	
Corrina Joseph - Live Your Life With Me (Original Mix)	
Nuyorican Soul - Nervous Track (Un Mix)
Blaze - Klubtrance
Ananda Project feat. Gaelle - Cascades of Colour (Manoo's Tribute Remix)
Trinidadian Deep - Caribbean Love (Original Mix)	
Antonio Ocasio - Spirit Rise (Calor Vibe Mix)	
Noel Nanton feat Sacha  - Love & Sadness (Sorrow Mix)
Ambiance II Fusion - Come Touch Tomorrow

translation.content: "House music to me is about emotion… it's about how it moves you," says Suze Phaff, better known as Suze Ijó. Emerging from a thriving Dutch scene, she belongs to a generation of DJs reshaping dance culture from the ground up, restoring soul and musicality to the centre of house music. It's a conversation happening not just in her home town of Rotterdam, but globally, among kindred spirits like MUSCLECARS and Dee Diggs in New York, Errol and Alex Rita of Touching Bass in London, and OMOLOKO in Belo Horizonte. You could call Suze Ijó a deep house DJ, but it's actually much deeper than that. Her sets embody house in its most musical sense, rich in nimble percussion, woodwinds, calypso drums, gospel vocals and romantic string sections. It channels the jazz-inflected heartbeat of the East Coast, the breeze of Balearic shores and the light-footed rhythms of the Caribbean. As Suze Ijó elucidates in her Q&A below, her RA Podcast "hopefully feels like a loving embrace." Opening with Key Trunks Ensemble's eternal "Calypso of House (Paradise Mix)", she sets a buoyant and life-affirming tone that continues for the following 90 minutes. As the mix unfolds, her affinity for timelessness is clear: many selections hark back to the golden age of house, from Lonesome Echo Production's "Sweet Dream (Shrine Sweet Mix)" to the euphoric swell of Blaze''s "Klubtrance." Listening to RA.986, it's no surprise that The Loft—David Mancuso's legendary, much-missed New York party—serves as a key influence for Suze Ijó. "He wasn't confined to just one genre but would just play 'good music' in the right context," she explains. "He allowed the music to breathe." If ambient music is defined by its ability to breathe in and reflect its surroundings, then Suze Ijó's RA Podcast is ambient in the truest, most human sense: a deep, enveloping soundscape that feels like sunrise, like community, like home—wherever you may choose to listen. – Bella Aquilina What have you been up to recently? I just returned to Rotterdam after living in Rio de Janeiro for three months. It was one of the best things I did for myself in recent years, in terms of personal growth and inspiration. Now I'm picking back up the pieces where I left them off. Getting back into my own rhythm and routine. How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? This mix was recorded in my living room in Rotterdam using two turntables, XDJ-700s and an E&S DJR400 mixer. It's a love letter to the dancers, the people who stay until the very end. I imagined that last magic hour of a party, when only a few are left, the sound system is heated up and the music seems to lift everyone's spirits just a little higher—everyone is getting down. It's warm, it's sweet, it's positive and hopefully it feels like a loving embrace. What are you looking forward to in the near future? Taking long walks on a summer night. Spending quality time with friends and family. What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? First of all the liberation of Palestine, Congo, Sudan and Haiti all deserve our attention. What's happening aren't separate events. These are all connected to fascist systems rooted in patriarchy and capitalism. Other than that I think it's important to shine a light on attention itself. What are you paying attention to? How are you spending your time? What are you consuming and what's consuming you? In this world of distractions it's so easy to get lost in the sauce. Take care of your body, mind and spirit. What's one club or party that had a major impact on you as an artist? 360 Degrees at BIRD in Rotterdam. An event that used to be organised by Guido van Dieren, a DJ and programmer from Rotterdam. Guido has been really influential to me in developing my musical ear. He was always booking amazing artists from all over the world like Theo Parrish, Sassy J, Lakuti & Tama Sumo and Joe Claussell. I used to work as the producer of the party ten years ago and working behind the scenes really introduced me to a whole new world. You've said, "House is a feeling, not a BPM range." Could you expand on that—what does "house" mean to you on a deeper level? House to me is about emotion, community and carries so many genres within itself. It's not confined to a tempo, it's about how it moves you. House can be joyful, melancholic, sensual or spiritual. It's the space where I feel free, connected and seen. You've mentioned the influence of The Loft and David Mancuso—what parts of that ethos do you try to carry with you when you DJ? When I first started collecting records, I was obsessed with The Loft, Paradise Garage, that whole era. I was constantly reading, watching, learning and trying to understand the spirit behind it. What inspired me the most about David Mancuso's work is that he quite literally opened up his home to people. He cared deeply about sound quality, about intention and community. He wasn't confined to just one genre but would just play "good music" in the right context. He allowed the music to breathe and instead of mixing he cared more about the "story." I used to be part of a collective here in Rotterdam. We shared a space that was inspired by The Loft. It's where I first started getting more serious about DJing and I'm eternally grateful for that time. I collected and played all types of records from spiritual jazz to Italian library music, to house and disco on two turntables and a rotary mixer. It didn't matter the genre or the tempo and I think this really helped me craft my style, sound and way of playing. Those parties still have a special place in my heart. To this day my sound has a strong connection to the past and I always include lots of nods to those musical pioneers who came before me. What's something you've experienced in Brazil that's changed the way you think about music or connection? First of all: how easy it was for me to pick up a new language. I always love to learn new skills and I feel like getting to know more about the music and culture and connecting with people speaking Portuguese has helped me a lot. I still have a lot of processing to do from my last trip but in general I would say: rhythm, joy and unity. I never encountered a place where music is such a big part of everyday life. It's such a powerful feeling when a certain song comes on and literally everyone sings along. It reminded me once again of how healing music and dance can be. How has Rotterdam shaped your musical outlook? Rotterdam used to have an amazingly rich nightlife scene. I started going out when I was a teenager so my first introduction were teenage parties during daytime in clubs and more traditional dancing parties with bands and Caribbean music. Later I moved on to the "grown-up" clubs and festivals. Rotterdam is a very multicultural city so I grew up with lots of different sounds and rhythms from kuduro to R&B, from bubbling to house, kaboela and hardcore. I think I've been very lucky to witness this melting pot of sounds and I think all these influences have made me a person who's open minded in my approach to music. It's the place where I first started working in nightlife, from flyering, door sales, bars, event production, artist handling, programming and eventually DJing and producing. I did it all.
translation.blurb: Deep house with heaps of soul to guide spring into summer.
